title: Partial Derivatives
description: Introduction In single-variable calculus, the derivative ūĚĎĎ ūĚĎ¶ ūĚĎĎ ūĚĎ• dx dy ‚Äč tells us the rate of change of y with respect to x. But neural networks have millions of parameters. A loss function might depend on weights ūĚĎ§ 1 , ūĚĎ§ 2 , ‚Ä¶ , ūĚĎ§ 1000000 w 1 ‚Äč ,w 2 ‚Äč ,‚Ä¶,w 1000000 ‚Äč . We can't simply ask "what's the slope?" because the function slopes differently in every direction. Instead, we ask: "How does the output change if I wiggle just one input while holding all others constant?" Why Partial Derivatives Matter Gradient Descent: The gradient is a vector of partial derivatives. Backpropagation: Computing partials for every weight in a neural network. Optimization: Finding which direction reduces loss the most. Sensitivity Analysis: Which inputs affect outputs the most? Formal Definition Let ūĚĎď ( ūĚĎ• , ūĚĎ¶ ) f(x,y) be a function of two variables. The partial derivative with respect to x is: ‚ąā ūĚĎď ‚ąā ūĚĎ• = lim ‚Ā° ‚Ąé ‚Üí 0 ūĚĎď ( ūĚĎ• + ‚Ąé , ūĚĎ¶ ) ‚ąí ūĚĎď ( ūĚĎ• , ūĚĎ¶ ) ‚Ąé ‚ąāx ‚ąāf ‚Äč =lim h‚Üí0 ‚Äč h f(x+h,y)‚ąíf(x,y) ‚Äč Note the ‚ąā ‚ąā symbol (partial) instead of d. This explicitly indicates that y is held constant. Similarly, the partial with respect to y is: ‚ąā ūĚĎď ‚ąā ūĚĎ¶ = lim ‚Ā° ‚Ąé ‚Üí 0 ūĚĎď ( ūĚĎ• , ūĚĎ¶ + ‚Ąé ) ‚ąí ūĚĎď ( ūĚĎ• , ūĚĎ¶ ) ‚Ąé ‚ąāy ‚ąāf ‚Äč =lim h‚Üí0 ‚Äč h f(x,y+h)‚ąíf(x,y) ‚Äč The Key Insight When computing ‚ąā ūĚĎď ‚ąā ūĚĎ• ‚ąāx ‚ąāf ‚Äč , treat y as if it were a constant number (like 5 or pi). Then differentiate with respect to x using your normal single-variable rules. Notation & Computation Several notations are used for partial derivatives: ‚ąā ūĚĎď ‚ąā ūĚĎ• ‚ąāx ‚ąāf ‚Äč Leibniz ūĚĎď ūĚĎ• f x ‚Äč Subscript ‚ąā ūĚĎ• ūĚĎď ‚ąā x ‚Äč f Operator ūĚź∑ ūĚĎ• ūĚĎď D x ‚Äč f D-notation Worked Example Let ūĚĎď ( ūĚĎ• , ūĚĎ¶ ) = ūĚĎ• 2 ūĚĎ¶ + 3 ūĚĎ¶ 3 + 5 f(x,y)=x 2 y+3y 3 +5. Find all partial derivatives. Partial with respect to x: Treat y as constant: ‚ąā ūĚĎď ‚ąā ūĚĎ• = 2 ūĚĎ• ūĚĎ¶ + 0 + 0 = 2 ūĚĎ• ūĚĎ¶ ‚ąāx ‚ąāf ‚Äč =2xy+0+0=2xy Partial with respect to y: Treat x as constant: ‚ąā ūĚĎď ‚ąā ūĚĎ¶ = ūĚĎ• 2 + 9 ūĚĎ¶ 2 + 0 = ūĚĎ• 2 + 9 ūĚĎ¶ 2 ‚ąāy ‚ąāf ‚Äč =x 2 +9y 2 +0=x 2 +9y 2 Geometric Intuition Consider a 3D surface ūĚĎß = ūĚĎď ( ūĚĎ• , ūĚĎ¶ ) z=f(x,y) representing a landscape with hills and valleys. Standing at a point (x, y), you can walk in infinitely many directions. Slicing the Surface When we compute ‚ąā ūĚĎď ‚ąā ūĚĎ• ‚ąāx ‚ąāf ‚Äč , we "slice" the 3D surface with a vertical plane where y = constant. This slice creates a 2D curve. The partial derivative is the slope of this curve at our point. Walking East-West ‚ąā ūĚĎď ‚ąā ūĚĎ• ‚ąāx ‚ąāf ‚Äč tells you the slope if you walk strictly in the x-direction (East-West). ‚ąā ūĚĎď ‚ąā ūĚĎ¶ ‚ąāy ‚ąāf ‚Äč tells you the slope if you walk strictly in the y-direction (North-South). The Gradient Vector The gradient collects all partial derivatives into a single vector. For a function ūĚĎď : ūĚĎÖ ūĚĎõ ‚Üí ūĚĎÖ f:R n ‚ÜíR: ‚ąá ūĚĎď = [ ‚ąā ūĚĎď ‚ąā ūĚĎ• 1 ‚ąā ūĚĎď ‚ąā ūĚĎ• 2 ‚čģ ‚ąā ūĚĎď ‚ąā ūĚĎ• ūĚĎõ ] ‚ąáf= ‚Äč ‚ąāx 1 ‚Äč ‚ąāf ‚Äč ‚ąāx 2 ‚Äč ‚ąāf ‚Äč ‚čģ ‚ąāx n ‚Äč ‚ąāf ‚Äč ‚Äč ‚Äč The symbol ‚ąá ‚ąá is called "nabla" or "del". Direction of Steepest Ascent The gradient ‚ąá ūĚĎď ‚ąáf points in the direction where f increases fastest. If you're standing on a hill, the gradient points uphill. Gradient Descent To minimize f (like a loss function), move in the opposite direction: ūĚúÉ ūĚĎõ ūĚĎí ūĚĎ§ = ūĚúÉ ūĚĎú ūĚĎô ūĚĎĎ ‚ąí ūĚõľ ‚ąá ūĚĎď őł new ‚Äč =őł old ‚Äč ‚ąíőĪ‚ąáf Magnitude = Steepness The magnitude ‚ą£ ‚ą£ ‚ąá ūĚĎď ‚ą£ ‚ą£ ‚ą£‚ą£‚ąáf‚ą£‚ą£ tells you how steep the slope is. Large gradient = steep terrain = big updates. Interactive: The Gradient For the function ūĚĎď ( ūĚĎ• , ūĚĎ¶ ) = ūĚĎ• 2 + ūĚĎ¶ 2 f(x,y)=x 2 +y 2 , the gradient is: ‚ąá ūĚĎď = [ ‚ąā ūĚĎď ‚ąā ūĚĎ• ‚ąā ūĚĎď ‚ąā ūĚĎ¶ ] = [ 2 ūĚĎ• 2 ūĚĎ¶ ] ‚ąáf=[ ‚ąāx ‚ąāf ‚Äč ‚ąāy ‚ąāf ‚Äč ‚Äč ]=[ 2x 2y ‚Äč ] Drag the point to see how the gradient always points toward steepest ascent (away from the minimum at the origin). Interactive Gradient f(x, y) = x¬≤ + y¬≤ ‚ąá ūĚĎď = ‚ü® 2 ūĚĎ• , 2 ūĚĎ¶ ‚ü© ‚ąáf=‚ü®2x,2y‚ü© min Click & Drag anywhere Partials = Components ‚ąā ūĚĎď ‚ąā ūĚĎ• = 2 ūĚĎ• ‚ąāx ‚ąāf ‚Äč =2x Horizontal Slope 2.00 ‚ąā ūĚĎď ‚ąā ūĚĎ¶ = 2 ūĚĎ¶ ‚ąāy ‚ąāf ‚Äč =2y Vertical Slope 2.00 GRADIENT CONSTRUCTION ‚ąá ūĚĎď = ‚ü® 2.00 , 2.00 ‚ü© ‚ąáf=‚ü®2.00,2.00‚ü© The gradient vector is literally just the list of partial derivatives. Magnitude (Steepness): 2.83 Direction: 45¬į Notice how the gradient always points perpendicular to the contour lines? Directional Derivatives Partial derivatives tell us the slope along coordinate axes. But what if we want to know the slope in an arbitrary direction? Directional Derivative The rate of change of f in direction of unit vector ūĚĎĘ u is: ūĚź∑ ūĚĎĘ ūĚĎď = ‚ąá ūĚĎď ‚čÖ ūĚĎĘ = ‚ą£ ‚ą£ ‚ąá ūĚĎď ‚ą£ ‚ą£ cos ‚Ā° ( ūĚúÉ ) D u ‚Äč f=‚ąáf‚čÖu=‚ą£‚ą£‚ąáf‚ą£‚ą£cos(őł) where ūĚúÉ őł is the angle between the gradient and direction u. Maximum When u = direction of gradient, cos(0) = 1. Maximum increase. Minimum When u = opposite of gradient, cos(180) = -1. Maximum decrease. Zero When u perpendicular to gradient, cos(90) = 0. Level curve (contour). Gradient is Perpendicular to Level Curves The gradient is always perpendicular to level curves (contours where f is constant). This is because moving along a level curve means zero change in f, which requires cos(theta) = 0. Higher-Order Partial Derivatives We can take partial derivatives of partial derivatives. These second-order partials are crucial for optimization (You can read more about it in the Hessian matrix). Second-Order Partials Pure second partial ‚ąā 2 ūĚĎď ‚ąā ūĚĎ• 2 = ‚ąā ‚ąā ūĚĎ• ( ‚ąā ūĚĎď ‚ąā ūĚĎ• ) ‚ąāx 2 ‚ąā 2 f ‚Äč = ‚ąāx ‚ąā ‚Äč ( ‚ąāx ‚ąāf ‚Äč ) Mixed partial ‚ąā 2 ūĚĎď ‚ąā ūĚĎ• ‚ąā ūĚĎ¶ = ‚ąā ‚ąā ūĚĎ• ( ‚ąā ūĚĎď ‚ąā ūĚĎ¶ ) ‚ąāx‚ąāy ‚ąā 2 f ‚Äč = ‚ąāx ‚ąā ‚Äč ( ‚ąāy ‚ąāf ‚Äč ) Clairaut's Theorem (Symmetry of Mixed Partials) If the mixed partials are continuous, then the order of differentiation doesn't matter: ‚ąā 2 ūĚĎď ‚ąā ūĚĎ• ‚ąā ūĚĎ¶ = ‚ąā 2 ūĚĎď ‚ąā ūĚĎ¶ ‚ąā ūĚĎ• ‚ąāx‚ąāy ‚ąā 2 f ‚Äč = ‚ąāy‚ąāx ‚ąā 2 f ‚Äč This is why the Hessian matrix is symmetric for most functions we encounter. ML Applications Backpropagation When training a neural network, we need ‚ąā ūĚźŅ ‚ąā ūĚĎ§ ūĚĎĖ ‚ąāw i ‚Äč ‚ąāL ‚Äč for every weight w_i. The chain rule lets us compute these efficiently by propagating gradients backward through the network. Automatic Differentiation PyTorch and TensorFlow build computation graphs and use the chain rule to compute all partial derivatives automatically. When you call loss.backward(), it computes the gradient of loss with respect to all parameters. Feature Importance (Saliency Maps) For a trained model, ‚ąā output ‚ąā input ūĚĎĖ ‚ąāinput i ‚Äč ‚ąāoutput ‚Äč tells us how sensitive the output is to each input feature. This creates "saliency maps" showing which pixels matter most for image classification. Regularization via Gradients Some regularization techniques penalize large gradients. For example, spectral normalization in GANs constrains the Lipschitz constant (maximum gradient magnitude) of the discriminator. Previous: Limits & Continuity Next: Chain Rule
